Why Wisdom Is A River

# Why Wisdom Is A River

### Living a good life by learning from the depths of nature

[](https://substack.com/@rasajournal)

![Garima Garg's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!h9ms!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2493377a-60dd-44d9-a41e-4aff3778a15c_660x1006.jpeg)

[Garima Garg](https://substack.com/@rasajournal)

Oct 30, 2025

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!n3Sy!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fbdd54e57-6680-4439-8a49-76c02f878e54_960x640.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!n3Sy!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fbdd54e57-6680-4439-8a49-76c02f878e54_960x640.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

*Somewhere in Kovalam, Kerala, in 2010. Photo by Garima Garg.*

One of my all-time favourite quotes is by the British writer, T.S. Eliot, from a play he wrote in 1934. One wonders what would he say about the modern times but even then, he was prescient enough to ask, “*where is the wisdom we have lost in knowledge, where is the knowledge we have lost in information*”? The play is not amongst his best-known works but these two sentences should definitely receive a lot more attention than they do.

Eliot passed away before the invention of home computers and internet, so what was he referring to really? Could it be that these words tug at something much deeper than what we now recognise as an overload of information, thanks to wifi-enabled smartphones which are easily affordable for the masses around the world now?

For the answers, we will have to go back much further than we might expect in order to understand the intellectual climate in which those words found utterance. Post the printing press revolution in 15th century, the Scientific Revolution in 16th century, the Enlightenment in 17th century, the ease of mass immigration in search of better economic opportunities as America built itself in 18th century, and the Gilded Age in 19th century— it was perhaps inevitable that a man born to a high society family in America would end up feeling disillusioned by the abundance of information, wealth, and rationality. If my conclusion feels rushed, let me now take a longer route to it.

In India, knowledge is a deity worshipped as Goddess Saraswati. She is the consort of the creator of Universe, Lord Brahma, and embodies the wisdom of books, music, and everything that is good and holy. She was named after the river that used to flow where Indus Valley Civilisation prospered thousands of years ago. In our secular age, we tend to take any instinct towards worship as a sign of feebleness or blind faith. But it is worth our while to explore why an ancient materially abundant civilisation chose to worship knowledge in form of a deity which was further symbolised by a river.

A tale from the Bhagavata Purana recounts how during the creation of the Universe, two miscreants stole the Vedas, i.e., knowledge, from Lord Brahma. They were called Madhu and Kaitabh. They were emboldened after they secured a life-insurance policy from Goddess Shakti but like all legal matters, they were undone by the fine print. In other words, they were killed off by Lord Vishnu at the behest of the goddess who granted them the boon. After this, the Vedas were secured with Lord Brahma and Goddess Saraswati once again.

A symbolic reading of the tale shows us that knowledge is a responsibility that needs to be handled with care. While it will always be sought by everyone, especially those who seek to create chaos by capitalising on it for personal gain, it must be guarded fiercely. In fact, the height of knowledge is wisdom and that can only be achieved when it is elevated as something higher than the mundane reality.

How does knowledge become wisdom? By mere worship of a deity? No. Our intellect is always sharper than that, by definition. So what can soften this intellect into something deeper and ever-flowing as a river? The answer is simple but the intellect desires something more complicated to feel validated in and of itself. Because when the intellect ponders the questions of existence, it comes to a point from where it cannot go any further. What carries us beyond that point? The wisdom of accepting that just like a river flows for the sake of it, life too must go on for its own sake.

Take, for example, the Nasadiya Sukta, also known as the Hymn of Creation, from the Rig Veda. Here is a translated version that I have copied from Wikipedia—

> Nasadiya Sukta (Hymn of non-Eternity, origin of universe):  
>   
> There was neither non-existence nor existence then;  
> Neither the realm of space, nor the sky which is beyond;  
> What stirred? Where? In whose protection?  
>   
> There was neither death nor immortality then;  
> No distinguishing sign of night nor of day;  
> That One breathed, windless, by its own impulse;  
> Other than that there was nothing beyond.  
>   
> Darkness there was at first, by darkness hidden;  
> Without distinctive marks, this all was water;  
> That which, becoming, by the void was covered;  
> That One by force of heat came into being;  
>   
> Who really knows? Who will here proclaim it?  
> Whence was it produced? Whence is this creation?  
> Gods came afterwards, with the creation of this universe.  
> Who then knows whence it has arisen?  
>   
> Whether God’s will created it, or whether He was mute;  
> Perhaps it formed itself, or perhaps it did not;  
> The Supreme Brahman of the world, all pervasive and all knowing  
> He indeed knows, if not, no one knows

—*[Rigveda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rigveda)* 10.129 (Abridged, Tr: Kramer / Christian)

While the hymn is breathtakingly beautiful in its entirety, it is the last verse that is the most interesting. It lays threadbare the human conundrum about existence in the simplest, yet the wisest words possible. Whether the existence has a creator or not, the intellect of ancient sages was subtle enough to come to a conclusion that it is possible that no one knows why existence exists.

That leads us to only one conclusion (or maybe two). For one, we can keep thinking about the nature of reality. But what I find more interesting is the idea of accepting the nature of reality— that it is what it is. The mind does not labour deeply only for the sake of curiosity but also to be liberated from life itself. Acceptance, however, comes from something else within our being and is more life-affirming. That, to me atleast, is true wisdom because instead of expanding the mind, it seeks to expand the soul. Where the mind recognises itself, it is the soul that realises itself. And while the mind moves in steps, the soul grows in leaps and bounds. Whatever the ultimate cosmic or our individual realities may be, a wholesome picture can emerge only when we fully immerse ourselves in it. That means engaging not just our mind but also our senses.

After all, why is it that the same deity represents both knowledge and art as well as books and speech? We cannot feed our mind without also feeding our senses and neither can we elevate our senses without refining our conscious thinking. The books may feed our mind but our senses are fed by nature. The nature that is the sun, the moon, the flowers, the wind, the fire, and the snow, as well as human emotions and speech. Little wonder, then, that the poets should write paeans to moon, singers sing the songs of joy and sorrow, and painters paint the landscapes of nature and the life around us. What knowledge helps us see, wisdom helps us experience.

For most part of human history, this was common sense. Traditional societies around the world emphasised these realities in their specific ways but the underlying wisdom remained the same. However, with the advent of widely available print books (as opposed to zealously guarded and limited amount of scribe-written books), we began to retreat within our minds. We began to privilege the perspective of the mind over the experience of the senses because we found it more “scientific”. We began to divorce ourselves from nature and eventually, the community. That meant a greater desire for personal material abundance, which further led to deeper emotional labyrinths.

Our atomisation reflects not just in our nuclear, even single-parent families now, but also in our awareness of reality itself. We seek neither the depths of nature nor the abundance of emotional meaning. For us, wisdom does not exist anymore and neither does knowledge. All we have now is bite-sized information, coming at us one 20-second reel or an AI-summary at a time.

The good news is that we can always reclaim our wisdom any time we want. The tough news is that wisdom is much tougher today than it has ever been for we now live not in knowledge but in deeper and deeper delusions of it.
